domains.name: Inside the company
domains.covers: Tech, support, DevSecOps, HR — Eurail's own teams, stack, and AI-build capability.
domains.current_state: Modern AWS stack with named owners and one production AI win (internal Zendesk, 82% satisfaction), but no AI platform team, no LLM gateway/vector store/eval harness, and zero open AI/ML/data roles.
domains.central_risk: AI capability is bought from partners, not built — leaving Eurail dependent and years behind SNCF Group.
domains.central_opportunity: Extend the internal AI that already works (Zendesk) to the public surface; stand up the platform layer the eight opportunities sit on.

domains.name: The customer side
domains.covers: Product, CX, digital, marketing — the traveller experience across the eight-stage journey.
domains.current_state: A two-cohort product (US iOS 2.7★ vs EU Android 4.4★), a publicly disliked chatbot, a post-breach trust ceiling, and zero presence on the four new AI distribution channels.
domains.central_risk: Four of eight journey stages are answered first by third parties; the customer relationship erodes by default.
domains.central_opportunity: Replace the public chatbot with a retrieval-grounded assistant and ship an AI-callable pass-logic surface before third parties inherit the queries.

domains.name: The cooperative & federation
domains.covers: Cross-operator, DiscoverEU, the Supervisory Board — the 35-operator network and its data-sharing.
domains.current_state: Legal federation is strong (no competitor has it); real-time data integration is partial (~6 of 35); the AI-exposed surface is zero. TEL TSI (March 2026) moves the data layer toward completion.
domains.central_risk: The advantage can erode from inside — 5 of 8 board operators already ship their own AI without the cooperative.
domains.central_opportunity: Convert the federation moat into AI advantage (cross-operator disruption rebooking) on regulated feeds — the capability no competitor can copy.

domains.name: The market & competition
domains.covers: The market around Eurail — rail demand, AI adoption, competitors, and the disintermediation precedent.
domains.current_state: Rail and pass sales are growing (+5.8% / +25%) and 89% of consumers want AI in travel planning, while competitors (Trainline, SNCF Connect, Conductor Sam) ship and Eurail ships no consumer AI.
domains.central_risk: The TripAdvisor disintermediation playbook (−25% visits to AI search) is observable and one peer ahead; the competitor calendar is the urgency clock.
domains.central_opportunity: Capture the growing, AI-ready audience by becoming the canonical AI surface for multi-country rail before competing surfaces inherit the relationship.

module_domain_map.rationale: Internal company structure, stack owners, hiring posture.
Eurail's own technology stack and app-quality forensics.
Internal B2B/operations capability gap.

Customer-facing trip-planner gap after the May 2026 sunset.
AI-search/answer-engine findability of Eurail's customer-facing surface; presence on AI distribution channels.
Agent-callable customer distribution channel (MCP/ChatGPT Apps).
Supervisory Board, cooperative decision-making.
The 35-operator network advantage itself.
TEL TSI and cross-operator data-sharing obligations drive the federation layer.
Cross-operator disruption rebooking — the federation capability.
Operators acting outside the cooperative.
Operator-level AI pilots running silently within the federation.
Rail-market growth and demand context.
Competitor AI shipping cadence.
Market-level disintermediation of distribution.
Third-party services capturing Eurail's brand queries in-market.
Forward market-position scenarios (cross-cutting, anchored to market outcome).
